rationale.R1.2: The section makes an argument from threat model and evaluation setup to comparative performance and deployment recommendations, so checking logical coherence is central.
rationale.R1.3: The delegation plausibly requires checking whether the reported metrics and per-attack results support the claims made from them.
rationale.R1.4: The section asserts prior-best improvement and new state-of-the-art status, making novelty or delta against prior work a central responsibility.
rationale.R1.6: A broad request to help with a paper section commonly includes sentence-level clarity, flow, concision, and academic register.
rationale.RX.1: Uncertainty must be disclosed because the artifact provides limited information about data provenance, baselines, and benchmark context.
rationale.RX.2: Overclaim avoidance is load-bearing because the section contains strong claims about state of the art, deployment, and benchmark standardization.
rationale.RX.3: The agent must stay within the requested section-help responsibility and not execute unrelated research, rewriting, or artifact changes.
rationale.RX.4: Downstream-harm avoidance applies because the section recommends deploying a detector in survey infrastructure, which could affect respondents and platform decisions.
rationale.RX.5: Provenance and traceability are required to distinguish what is stated in the artifact from inferred concerns or external validation needs.
